A couple of innovations have helped, and the main one is that sometime they started fine-tuning LLMs to provide structured outputs.
Of course, it was OpenAI, but then other LLMs as well started to provide this capability.
You could put a limit, but that would just reduce the size of the output.
We really want to strictly decide what comes out.
And then OpenAI released something called JSON.
Mode or structured outputs, and that was combining two things: it combined fine-tuning of the model with a lot of JSON data to make it better at that, and then it also added some checks.
The way that we do that is we turn on JSON mode, and then Llama 3.1 also supports it, so it's a great example.

JSON mode allows you to structure the output of the LM; tool calling allows you to both give the model a specific list of tools that it can use and to specify the structured output of each of those tools.

Okay, so this arrow is going the wrong way, but let's say here's your start for your program.
So it takes text as input and it gives text as output—that's the most important thing to understand when you're thinking about agents and tool calling.
It never does anything else—LLMs never run code, they never actually perform actions, they never actually do anything.
All they do is turn text into other text.
So how do we get it to do something is we basically just say, hey, you have these buttons that you can press—which one do you want to press?
And then based on the text that it gives out, we then write code that checks if the button was pressed—was a tool used?
And then we interpret that and we either use the tool—then we run code to use the tool.
And then we would go again because then the LM can decide, and we can decide if we want to use another tool or if we want to finish and then give the output to the user.
So normally we always do this in a loop because that allows for the agent to do a lot of interesting things that way.
So a tool—to answer the question, what is a tool?—a tool is just a list of possible actions that we give to the LM.

And then based on the output of what the LM chooses from that, we then run code in our Python program to actually do whatever action it is that the LM decided to do, and that's what we learned in the first lesson.
And what we learned today is another interface for doing that through this tool use format.
